title: Structural verification checks
short_description: Implement the always-on structural layer: REF.* reference resolution with nearest-match hints, ID uniqueness, timing-closure re-checks, animation-outside-visibility-window detection, and BUILD.EXCEPTION capture with file/line so no raw traceback ever reaches an agent.
long_description_md:
# Structural verification checks

## Why this task exists

`anima verify` is the product's entire honesty claim: "verify passes" must mean
something, or the whole pitch (an agent ships a 15-20 minute video without a human
watching it) collapses into vibes. Structural checks are the foundation the other two
verification layers (layout/readability, taste constraints — separate Phase 2/5 tasks)
stand on: they are the **always-on, error-severity** layer per product-spec capability 7
— "scene-graph validity (unique IDs, resolvable references, assets exist, fonts
available), timing arithmetic (beat durations sum to scene durations; every animated
property resolves to exactly one value at every t; no animation targets a node outside
its visibility window)". If this layer is wrong or incomplete, everything built on top of
it inherits a false sense of correctness, and the layout/readability/taste rules end up
computing geometry over a graph that was never actually structurally sound.

This task also carries the NFR's single most load-bearing reliability requirement:
*"Failure = finding: any foreseeable failure (bad reference, missing asset, invalid
timing) surfaces as a structured finding with a fix hint. Raw tracebacks reaching the
user are product bugs."* An agent that gets a Python traceback instead of a `Finding`
cannot programmatically recover — it has no stable code to pattern-match, no `fix_hint`,
nothing to feed back into its next edit. `BUILD.EXCEPTION` capture is what stands between
"the product's core promise" and "an agent staring at a stack trace it can't parse."

## Roadmap fit

`docs/cofounder/ROADMAP.md`, **Phase 2: Verification engine v1 + subtitles**, done-when
bullet 1, verbatim: *"Structural checks complete: `REF.*`, ID uniqueness, timing closure
invariants, animation-outside-visibility, `BUILD.EXCEPTION` capture with file/line (no
raw tracebacks)."* That is this task's exact scope. The sibling Phase 2 bullets —
layout/readability rules (`OVERLAP.*`, `MARGIN.*`, `FONT.MIN_SIZE`, `TIMING.READ_TIME`,
`CONTRAST.LOW`), the VerificationReport schema and render gating, burned-in subtitles,
verify performance budgets, and the agent-in-the-loop regression suite — are separate
Phase 2 tasks that either this task depends on (the schema) or that build on top of what
ships here (everything else). Map validity checks (`MAP.UNKNOWN_TERRITORY`,
`MAP.ARROW_OUT_OF_VIEW`, `MAP.DATE_MISMATCH`) are explicitly **not** this task's scope —
per ROADMAP Phase 4 ("Map engine"), they ship once the map dataset and territory index
exist; this task's `REF.*` mechanism is deliberately built so the Phase 4 map task can
plug territory-id resolution into the same nearest-match machinery rather than
reinventing it, but no territory/dataset-aware code is written here.

Depends on:
- **`verification-report-schema`** — every structural check in this task produces
  `Finding` objects (namespaced `code`, `severity="error"` — structural checks are the
  always-on error layer, no warnings), assembled into a `VerificationReport`. This task
  builds *rule logic*; it consumes the schema, code-registry, and deterministic-ordering
  contract that task already established rather than inventing a parallel shape.
- **`timeline-compiler`** — timing-closure and animation-visibility re-checks operate on
  the compiled `Track`/`Keyframe` output (per `data-model.md`: "Beat 1-n Action --compiles
  to--> Track n-1 (Node, property)"), not on raw authored beats. This task consumes that
  compiler's output object model to independently recompute the invariants the compiler
  is supposed to already guarantee at construction time (belt-and-suspenders, per
  `data-model.md` invariant 1). `scene-graph-model` is not listed as a direct dependency
  because it is already transitively required by both `verification-report-schema` and
  `timeline-compiler`; this task's `REF.*` checks walk the graph objects that
  `timeline-compiler`'s build step already produces alongside the compiled tracks.
- **`font-bundling`** — `REF.UNKNOWN_FONT_ROLE` checks resolve font-role references
  against the bundled font manifest that task defines. Without it there is no real
  namespace to check font roles against, only a guessed one.
- `REF.UNKNOWN_STYLE_ROLE` is OUT of scope here: `style_role` namespace is defined by
  `style-pack-system` (Phase 3), so that check ships with `style-pack-system`, not this
  Phase 2 task. Keeps phase ordering intact — this task never depends on Phase 3 work.

## The four check families

All findings from this layer are `severity="error"` — product-spec capability 7 is
explicit that structural checks are the always-on error layer (unlike layout/readability
and taste, which mix errors and warnings). A project with any structural finding never
proceeds to render, per the render-gate policy `verification-report-schema` defines.

### 1. Build-time exception capture (`BUILD.EXCEPTION`)

Wraps the graph-construction step (authored Python -> `Project`/`Scene`/`Layer`/`Node`
tree, per `data-model.md`'s "Project build" lifecycle) in a boundary that never lets a
raw exception propagate to the CLI or the agent. Two cases:

- **Known structural errors from `scene-graph-model`** (duplicate/collision IDs,
  inconsistent layer membership, malformed subtype payloads, unknown discriminators) are
  typed exceptions that already carry the offending ID and model path per that task's
  contract. This task catches them and translates each into the correct namespaced
  `Finding` (e.g. `REF.DUPLICATE_ID`, `REF.INCONSISTENT_LAYER`) rather than a generic
  `BUILD.EXCEPTION` — a known, categorized failure gets a specific code, not a catch-all.
- **Any other exception** raised while executing the project's authoring code or during
  graph construction (a bug in the agent's own scene script — bad arithmetic, a
  misused API, an unhandled `ValueError`) is caught at the outermost build boundary and
  converted into a single `BUILD.EXCEPTION` finding. The finding's `fix_hint` and
  `actual` fields must include: the exception type and message, and a `file`/`line`
  pointing at the **user's project source**, not `anima` internals — this requires
  walking the traceback and selecting the innermost frame whose filename is inside the
  project directory (falling back to the outermost user-code frame if none match),
  exactly as the NFR demands ("Raw tracebacks reaching the user are product bugs").
  No Python traceback text is ever included verbatim in the finding; only the
  structured type/message/file/line extracted from it.

### 2. `REF.*` reference resolution with nearest-match hints

Per `data-model.md` invariant 3 ("Referential integrity: every `style_role`, font role,
asset id, territory id, node id reference resolves; ids unique... violations are `REF.*`
findings"), this check walks the fully-built graph plus the beat/action layer and
confirms every reference resolves against the actual available namespace at this phase
of the project:

- Node-id references used by actions/animations/camera declarations that target a node
  by ID resolve to an actual node in the project (`REF.UNKNOWN_NODE`).
- `style_role` references resolve against the pinned style pack's declared role
  namespace available at this phase (`REF.UNKNOWN_STYLE_ROLE`) — palette/asset
  *content* resolution is Phase 3 (style packs) scope; this task checks the role
  *name* against the pack's declared role schema, which is available now.
- Font-role references resolve against the bundled font roles from Phase 0
  (`REF.UNKNOWN_FONT_ROLE`).
- Group-child and layer-membership references are re-validated defensively even though
  `scene-graph-model` already enforces them at construction (belt-and-suspenders,
  matching the timing-closure re-check posture) — if construction-time enforcement is
  ever bypassed (e.g. a graph built from deserialized JSON instead of the authoring
  API), verify must still catch it rather than silently trusting the input.

Every `REF.*` finding's `fix_hint` includes a **nearest-match suggestion**: a bounded
edit-distance (Levenshtein or equivalent) search over the valid namespace for that
reference kind (all live node IDs, all declared style roles, all bundled font roles),
surfacing the closest match(es) within a configurable threshold ("did you mean
`label_7`?") and falling back to "no close match; valid options are: [...]" (truncated
if the namespace is large) when nothing is close enough to be useful. This is the same
nearest-match mechanism ROADMAP Phase 4 explicitly calls out reusing for
`MAP.UNKNOWN_TERRITORY` — building it generically here (keyed on an arbitrary "valid
namespace" input, not hardcoded to node IDs) avoids that duplication when territory
resolution lands.

### 3. Timing-closure and visibility-window re-checks

Independent, verify-side recomputation of `data-model.md` invariant 1, operating on the
compiler's actual output (not by re-deriving the compiler's internal logic, but by
checking its *results* against the arithmetic they claim to satisfy):

- Recompute `scene.duration == sum(beat.duration for beat in scene.beats)` and
  `project.duration == sum(scene.duration for scene in project.scenes) + transitions`
  from the compiled timeline; any drift is `TIMING.CLOSURE_MISMATCH` with expected vs.
  actual values.
- For every animated `(node, property)` track, confirm the compiled keyframes resolve to
  **exactly one value** at every sampled t across the track's domain — no gaps
  (`TIMING.UNRESOLVED_VALUE`, a t with no defined value) and no ambiguous overlaps
  (`TIMING.OVERLAPPING_KEYFRAMES`, two keyframes claiming the same t on the same
  property without a defined resolution order).
- For every track/action with a defined time window, confirm that window falls entirely
  inside its target node's resolved `visible_window` — an animation attempting to run
  before entry or after exit is `ANIMATION.OUTSIDE_VISIBILITY`, naming the node, the
  animation's time window, and the node's actual visibility window.

This is explicitly belt-and-suspenders per `data-model.md` ("Enforced by the timeline
compiler (construction) and re-checked by verify") — the check must not simply trust
that the compiler already got this right; it recomputes from the compiled track/graph
objects directly, so a compiler bug or a hand-constructed (non-authored) project still
gets caught.

### 4. Structural-checks entry point and report assembly

A single callable (e.g. `run_structural_checks(project) -> list[Finding]`) that runs all
three check families above in a deterministic, documented order, deduplicates where two
families could both flag the same underlying defect (e.g. a `BUILD.EXCEPTION` from a
failed construction short-circuits the other three families for that project, since
there is no valid graph to check further), and assembles the results into a
`VerificationReport` using the schema and deterministic-ordering contract from
`verification-report-schema`. This is the integration point the not-yet-built `anima
verify` CLI command will call; this task delivers the pure function, not the CLI wiring
(consistent with `verification-report-schema`'s explicit scope boundary that CLI
wiring is separate).

## Explicitly out of scope

- **Map validity checks** (`MAP.UNKNOWN_TERRITORY`, `MAP.ARROW_OUT_OF_VIEW`,
  `MAP.DATE_MISMATCH`, projection-defined checks) — ROADMAP Phase 4, once the map
  dataset and territory index exist. This task's nearest-match helper is built generic
  enough for that task to reuse, but no map-specific code ships here.
- **Layout/readability rules** (`OVERLAP.*`, `MARGIN.SAFE_AREA`, `FONT.MIN_SIZE`,
  `TIMING.READ_TIME`, `CONTRAST.LOW`) and **taste constraints** (`PACING.*`,
  `CONTINUITY.*`, `STYLE.PALETTE_VIOLATION`) — separate Phase 2 and Phase 5 tasks; they
  depend on the geometry resolver and style-pack constraint profiles respectively, and
  on this task's structural layer having already guaranteed the graph is sound enough to
  compute geometry over.
- **`VerificationReport`/`Finding` schema, code registry, human formatter, and render
  gating** — built by `verification-report-schema`; this task only produces `Finding`
  instances against that contract.
- **The `anima verify` CLI command itself** (argument parsing, `--json` output,
  stdout/stderr separation, typed exit codes) — a future CLI-integration task wires this
  task's `run_structural_checks` entry point into the actual command.
- **Style-pack content resolution** (actual palette colors, asset manifests, license
  metadata) — Phase 3; this task only checks that a referenced *role name* exists in the
  pack's declared role namespace, not that the resolved visual content is correct.
- **Performance/spatial-indexing work** for evaluating rules at scale — the NFR's
  verify-time budgets (<=5s/10-beat, <=30s/100-beat) apply to the verification engine as
  a whole; this task's checks must be efficient (single graph/track walk, no repeated
  O(n^2) scans where an index/dict lookup suffices) but a dedicated spatial-index
  performance task is separate, in-roadmap scope for the geometry-heavy layout rules,
  not for this structural layer.

deliverables.description: Build-boundary exception capture: wraps project graph construction so scene-graph-model's typed structural errors (duplicate/collision IDs, inconsistent layer membership, malformed subtype payloads, unknown discriminators) are translated into specific namespaced Findings, and any other exception raised during authoring/construction is caught and converted into a single BUILD.EXCEPTION finding carrying exception type/message and a file/line pointing at the user's project source (never anima-internal frames, never a raw traceback string).

REF.* reference resolution over the built graph and its beat/action layer: unknown node-id targets, unknown style_role names, and unknown font-role names each raise a distinct REF.* Finding; every such finding's fix_hint includes a bounded edit-distance nearest-match suggestion against the actual valid namespace when one exists within threshold, and a truncated valid-options fallback when none is close enough. Defensive re-validation of group-child/layer-membership consistency is included as belt-and-suspenders coverage.

Timing-closure and visibility-window re-checks computed independently from the compiled timeline: scene/project duration closure (TIMING.CLOSURE_MISMATCH on drift), exactly-one-resolved-value-at-every-t per animated (node, property) track (TIMING.UNRESOLVED_VALUE / TIMING.OVERLAPPING_KEYFRAMES), and animation time windows contained within their target node's resolved visibility window (ANIMATION.OUTSIDE_VISIBILITY) -- each with fixture projects that pass and fixture projects that violate the specific invariant.

run_structural_checks(project) entry point running all three check families in deterministic order (short-circuiting to BUILD.EXCEPTION-only output when construction itself fails), assembling results into a VerificationReport via the verification-report-schema contract with all structural findings at severity=error, exercised end-to-end against a library of fixture projects (one clean-pass project, one per violation kind across all four families) with full lint/type checks on the new module.

verification.command: pytest -q tests/verification/test_structural_checks_integration.py && ruff check src/anima/verification tests/verification && mypy --strict src/anima/verification
